ingredients.prep: for simmering meat (add more as needed)
optional but classic in some styles
optional; reserved cooking fat or softened lard for moisture

steps.text: Prep casings (if stuffing): Rinse casings under cool water, then soak per package directions. Flush water through each casing to clean the inside. Keep submerged in clean water until ready to stuff.
Cook pork: In a large pot, add pork shoulder, onion, celery, bell pepper, garlic, bay leaves, salt, and black pepper. Cover with water. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook until pork is very tender.
Add liver near the end: If using pork liver or chicken livers, add them during the last 20–30 minutes of cooking so they cook through without becoming overly tough.
Cook rice: While meat simmers, cook rice separately (or use some of the strained broth for extra flavor). Cook until tender; fluff and set aside.
Strain and reserve broth: Remove meat and aromatics from the pot. Strain the cooking liquid and reserve several cups as broth for mixing. Discard bay leaves and any tough vegetable bits.
Grind/chop meat and liver: When cool enough to handle, grind pork and liver through a coarse plate, or pulse in a food processor. Aim for a rustic texture (not a smooth paste).
Mix filling: In a large bowl, combine ground pork/liver, cooked rice, Cajun seasoning, cayenne, thyme, optional sage, green onions, and parsley. Add pork fat (if using) for moisture.

Adjust texture with broth: Add reserved broth a little at a time and mix until the filling is moist and cohesive—soft enough to stuff and juicy when cooked, but not soupy. Taste and adjust salt/heat as needed.
Stuff (optional): Load the mixture into a sausage stuffer. Slide casing onto the tube and tie off the end. Stuff gently, keeping the casing full but not tight. Twist into links.
Poach links: Bring a pot of water (or reserved broth) to a bare simmer (not boiling). Poach boudin links until heated through. Avoid boiling to prevent casings from bursting.
Serve: Serve warm as links (often eaten by squeezing filling from casing) or crisp the outside by grilling or baking briefly after poaching.
